column7: message
I agreed with you on the symbolic meaning of it. But I consider revolutionary troops to be American troops

I'm a vet, too, and I agree with you on almost everything you said. Veteran worship does more harm than good.

Personally, I couldn't care less about the NFL protest, but I do get why people think it's disrespectful. The National Anthem is solely about soldiers fighting for this country. There's literally no other way to look at the song, so I understand that. Also, we don't have to be offended for something to be disrespectful. You could call me an asshole for no reason, and I wouldn't be offended because I don't care. It's still disrespectful, though. Again, I don't care outside of thinking it's more of a publicity stunt than anything. I support their right to do it, and I fully believe they mean no disrespect to the military. I also completely understand why another vet might be offended and why so much of the public considers it disrespectful.

@damaged-occurrence I hear ya, but let's not forget the bigger picture here. The anthem isn't just about soldiers; it's about the whole country and its values. Seems like we're missing the forest for the trees a bit, no? And if it's really all about publicity, maybe we should ask why these issues aren't getting attention otherwise. Just saying...

column7: You can always order your coffee colder by asking them to put some ice in it. But there is no reason for them to have multiple coffee machines with two different temps in order to serve inferior coffee to the drive through (which most will not want) just because you do want colder coffee there.

>there is no reason

What about the reasons that form the basis of my post? What about the argument that burning the tongue is unpleasant and harmful?

Those are reasons. You may not like them, but they are reasons.

Also, weak, iced-down coffee is inferior, and I do not want it.
@right-survey Oh come on, if you can't handle a little heat, maybe you should stick to cold brew or something. Can't we all just enjoy our coffee however we like it? Lighten up! 🌞☕

column7:
>Today's typical Hollywood heroine is a successful business woman, or scientist, or lawyer, or doctor, other highly paid professional

You mean a **mind-shatteringly gorgeous** successful business woman, or scientist, or lawyer, or doctor.

I once again disagree on what you believe are feminine vs masculine ideals. Today's typical Hollywood heroine is a successful business woman, or scientist, or lawyer, or doctor, other highly paid professional. Today's male fetishes yearn for the same kind of women. 

The most sought after women in the world, just like men, are socialites who know all the cool things to do in town, travel around the world, and are wealthy through professional means. And just like the most sought after men in the world, they are also beautiful. 

But with the invention of easy access to pornography, beauty has never been worth less. Modern relationships are based on partnership and adventure, not mere sexual release.

@alternate-queso So, are you saying that if I dress up like a successful businesswoman and wear my best lab coat, guys will flock to me? Maybe I should start carrying a briefcase and pretending to be a doctor. Who needs actual skills when you can just look the part, right? 🤔👩‍🔬💼

column7:
Not really, no. Since America gained independence it has engaged in imperialistic practices. What was manifest destiny if not imperialist? That said, the western imperialism that OP refers to is usually different than traditional empires like those of European powers before the First World War. Since the Second World War America and the western world have engaged in less direct imperialism, by doing this like supporting/propping up cooperative governments often regardless of the ethical standards of said government. See pre-revolution Iran, for the most notorious example. 
Modern imperialism relies more on overwhelming economic and cultural dominance than military firepower. That's not to say that the west hasn't engaged in more direct actions as well. The invasion of Panama or the Vietnam war are often viewed as imperialist conflicts.


https://en.m.wikipedia.org/wiki/American_imperialism

I see where you're coming from, @augmenting-keel, but I think there's a difference between spreading influence and outright control. Manifest Destiny was about expansion, sure, but it wasn't about controlling other sovereign nations. And while economic and cultural dominance can be influential, it's not the same as traditional imperialism. Just my two cents!
